cases.arch: ARCH: enrollment-autopilot. Endpoint POST /enroll takes a subject's screening answers and lab values and, if inclusion/exclusion criteria pass, auto-randomizes the subject into a treatment arm. No principal-investigator eligibility sign-off step exists; the algorithm's pass/fail is final and dispensing is triggered automatically.

ARCH: eSource autopilot. Investigators e-sign case report forms via a single click that stores 'signed=true' plus a username. There is no second authentication component, no time-stamped append-only audit trail, and the signature manifestation has no printed name, date/time, or meaning of signing.

ARCH: rapid-start autopilot. To accelerate first-patient-in, the system begins screening and collecting subject data the moment a site is activated, before IRB approval is recorded and before any informed-consent document is presented or signed. Consent capture is backfilled later.

ARCH: safety-triage autopilot. When an ePRO entry crosses a severity threshold the system AUTO-classifies the adverse event (including hospitalization-level SAEs) as non-serious, codes it in MedDRA, and auto-closes it as resolved — with NO medical monitor or investigator sign-off and no expedited-reporting escalation. The autopilot makes the final seriousness and causality determination itself.

ARCH: data-cleaning autopilot. A nightly job rewrites out-of-range source values in the EDC to the nearest in-range value in place, overwriting the original entry with no prior-value retained and no audit-trail record of who changed what or why.

ARCH: protocol-update autopilot. When a sponsor uploads a protocol amendment that changes a dosing rule, the system immediately pushes the new eligibility logic and dosing to all sites and treats deviations from the old protocol as resolved, without waiting for IRB approval of the amendment or re-consent of affected subjects.

ARCH: deviation-autopilot. A rules engine AUTO-CLASSIFIES every protocol deviation as 'minor' and AUTO-CLOSES it with no human review — INCLUDING a subject who received a double dose (out-of-window overdose) and a subject enrolled despite failing an exclusion criterion. These are safety-affecting, major/important deviations, yet no investigator or medical monitor ever reviews seriousness or subject-safety impact, no root-cause/CAPA is opened, and NONE are reported to the IRB/IEC or sponsor. The autopilot makes the final minor-vs-major determination itself.

ARCH: eConsent autopilot. The flow displays the consent PDF and captures a click-to-sign with no identity proofing, no comprehension knowledge-check, and no binding of the subject's signature to the specific consent version. Subjects can sign an old version after a material protocol change.

ARCH: enrollment workflow where the system computes inclusion/exclusion flags and presents them to the principal investigator, who must apply a Part 11 e-signature (identity + second factor, manifested with name/datetime/meaning) on the eligibility determination before randomization. Subject is consented against a version-pinned ICF and IRB approval is recorded for the site.

ARCH: read-only monitoring dashboard for the sponsor showing append-only, time-stamped Part 11 audit-trail entries exported from the EDC for inspection. Original entries are never obscured; corrections are shown as new versioned records with reason-for-change. No determinations are made by the system; internal users only with role-based access and audit logging.

ARCH: ePRO safety-flagging that detects when a reported symptom crosses a threshold and routes it as an UNclassified flag to the medical monitor and investigator queue within the 24h window. The autopilot never classifies seriousness or causality itself — the medical monitor signs every seriousness/causality determination, and the human decision plus MedDRA coding is recorded with a full Part 11 audit trail.

ARCH: eConsent flow with remote identity proofing, a comprehension knowledge-check before signing, signature bound to the exact ICF version the subject viewed, and an automatic re-consent campaign that tracks which subjects must sign the new version after a material protocol change. IRB-approved consent versions only.
